sections.text: Decision Log
FCCPS AI Policy Outline:
Policy Outline
Guiding Principles and Motivation
Virtues we’re trying to capture:
Informed by evidence
IB-aligned
Tailored to needs off different grade-bands
Sufficiently flexible and evolving to adapt to changing technology
Idealized Goals for Regulating GenAI in FC Schools:
Articulate general thinking on how allowing GenAI use supports:
The specific goal of AI literacy
Other school learning objectives
Articulate general thinking on how restricting certain GenAI use relates to:
The specific goal of AI literacy
Other school learning objectives
DECISION 1: While we acknowledge potential benefits from AI applications in the school system:
We see a lesser and less immediate need to develop policy to accelerate and promote GenAI adoption in the schools.
We see a greater and more immediate need to develop policy to protect learning by restricting certain GenAI applications in the schools.
Pragmatic Constraints on Restriction of GenAI Applications
We acknowledge limits on our ability to fully enforce GenAI restrictions when work is un-proctored
We pursue opportunities to move summative assessments into a proctored environment, but acknowledge constraints.
We value maintaining positive and trusting rapport between students and teachers
We are motivated to protect students from patently unfair outcomes:
False accusations of cheating
Competing with peers who are in rampant non-compliance with GenAI restrictions
DECISION 2: So we steer between two extremes:
Abandoning our idealized goals due to pragmatic constraints leads to race-to-the-bottom conditions. Students lose the freedom not to use GenAI.
Pursuing our idealized restrictions, at all costs, creates a climate of mistrust and fear that is its own impediment to learning
Scope & Triaging
DECISION 3: Limit Focus to Gen-AI and Gen-AI detectors?
DECISION 4: Cover non-academic Gen-AI risks? (i.e., mental-health risks, harassment risks)
Definitions
Restrictions on Staff & Teacher Use of AI
DECISION 5: Teachers allowed to use of GenAI in providing feedback, evaluating students?
DECISION 6: Allowed use of AI detectors of GenAI work product
DECISION 7: Use of GenAI in high-stakes school adjudicatory documents (discipline, IEP documents, etc)
Student Use
PreK-5
DECISION 8: Teach AI literacy while globally minimizing in-school AI use
Grades 6-12 DECISION 9: Consensus on use-cases and postures
Categories of Anticipated Student Use
Prohibited w/o administrative exception
Prohibited w/o teacher allowance
Allowed unless teacher prohibits
Citation and Disclosure Expectations
DECISION 10: Submission of chat logs required when Using GenAI on school assignments:
Protects students from false accusation
To allow teachers to get a window onto the actual GenAI use category
Implementation details TBD, maybe outside scope of policy
Flexibility for Students with Disabilities
Protection of Student Privacy
Public Accountability relating to use of AI by students and staff:
DECISION 11: How to scope commitments so that they are implementable?   Think about commitments to:
Public notice
Consideration of public comment
Parental opt-out
Equity in Our Community
Not sure how to frame this… here’s one possibility:
How can we prevent over-reliance on GenAI in a way that:
Ameliorates existing inequities in our community?
Avoids creating new inequities?
How can we structure access to GenAI tools in a way that:
Ameliorates existing inequities in our community?
Avoids creating new inequities?
School Budgeting/Workload Implications
AI impacts on teacher workload
AI impacts on teacher staffing levels
Reassessment and Updating of Policy
